nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to Lake Matano in the Malili lake system of South Sulawesi, where it appears to be evenly distributed through the lake's rocky shorelines and submerged cliffs. The lake is an ultra-oligotrophic ancient lake with very stable, warm, mildly alkaline water year-round. Listed as endangered on the IUCN Red List.

decorPreferences: smooth rocks, lava rock, and rubble (Lake Matano-style hardscape)
caves and crevices for burrowing and molting
fine sand substrate for burrow construction (2 inches / 5 cm or deeper)
driftwood and roots, including pieces emerging above the waterline as perches
tightly fitted lid — these are skilled escape artists
avoid soft live plants (will be nibbled or shredded)

diet.notes: The Sulawesi Orange-Arm Crab is an omnivorous scavenger with a strong carnivorous tendency — in the wild it is a specialized snail predator. In captivity it readily accepts sinking crab pellets, algae wafers, frozen bloodworms and mysis shrimp, blanched vegetables, and occasional pieces of fish or shrimp. Calcium supplementation through cuttlebone or mineral stones supports healthy molting. Feed once or twice daily and remove uneaten food within 24 hours to protect water quality. Avoid any food formulated with copper, as all freshwater crustaceans are highly sensitive to it.

goodWith: kept singly or as a single bonded male/female pair in a large tank
fast, upper-water fish that stay clear of the bottom (limited compatibility)
avoidWith: other male Matano Crabs (will fight, often fatally)
dwarf shrimp and Sulawesi Caridina (will be predated)
snails of any size (a preferred prey item in the wild)

summary: A fully-aquatic, deep-purple Sulawesi crab with contrasting pale joints and eyestalks. Endemic to Lake Matano, it requires warm, hard, alkaline Sulawesi-biotope water, a tightly sealed tank to prevent escape, and an experienced keeper.
careNotes: Syntripsa matannensis is an advanced-keeper species that shares the warm, mildly alkaline, parameter-stable demands of the Sulawesi Caridina shrimps it lives alongside in Lake Matano. Target water is typically remineralised RO/DI with a Sulawesi-specific mineral salt (e.g., Salty Shrimp Sulawesi Mineral 7.5/8.5) aiming for roughly TDS 100–150 ppm, pH 7.8–8.4, GH 6–10, KH 4–8, and 26–30°C. Sources broadly agree the species loses colour and becomes lethargic below about pH 7.8 — keep the lower end above this threshold even though wider tolerance ranges (7.0–8.5) appear in some sources. Tank size also varies in the literature: retailer minimums of 20 US gallons (~76 L) appear alongside specialist recommendations of 80 cm footprint or larger for a pair; a 100 L / 80 cm tank is a reasonable floor for a single animal, with more space strongly preferred when housing a pair. Sulawesi Orange-Arm Crabs are fully aquatic — no land area is required — but they will climb out of any open tank with remarkable agility, so a tightly-sealed lid is mandatory and heaters, airline, and filter outlets should not be used as escape ladders. They are territorial nocturnal hunters: males will fight other males to the death, and almost any dwarf shrimp, snail, slow-moving fish, or other invertebrate in the tank should be considered prey. Provide ample burrowing substrate (sand at least 5 cm / 2 inches deep) and a rocky hardscape with caves so the crab can excavate burrows and shelter during molts. Like all freshwater invertebrates, this species is highly copper-sensitive; never use copper-based medications or copper-containing foods. The IUCN lists S. matannensis as endangered; sourcing from reputable breeders or specialist importers is preferred over undocumented wild-collected stock.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is considered very difficult and is not reliably documented. Only sporadic, unverified accounts of mating in captivity exist, with no consistent reports of successful egg development or juvenile rearing. Sexing adults is straightforward — males are larger and bear noticeably larger chelae than females of comparable age. Hobbyists wishing to attempt breeding should keep a bonded pair in a spacious, parameter-stable Sulawesi-biotope tank with abundant burrows and minimal disturbance, but should treat any offspring as a bonus rather than an expected outcome.

primary.name: Aquarium Breeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder)
primary.notes: Primary swap: assigned primary Flip Aquatics homepage (https://flipaquatics.com/) had no species page for this crab, and the assigned secondary Practical Fishkeeping (https://www.practicalfishkeeping.co.uk/) returned HTTP 403. The Aquarium Breeder dedicated species guide provided the most complete and consistent dataset across all required fields and was promoted to primary.

additional.name: Garnelio (Purple Matano Crab product page)
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (24–30°C, pH 7.0–8.5, GH 5–15, KH 3–10), adult size up to ~7 cm, 80 cm tank for a pair, fully-aquatic setup with above-water perches, territorial behaviour, snail/shrimp predation, and a lifespan estimate of 4–6 years (higher than Aquarium Breeder's ~3 years — averaged into the 3–5 year figure used here).

additional.name: Liam Hopkins — Sulawesi/Matano Crab Care Guide (Revised)
additional.notes: Hobbyist specialist guide. Cross-checked aggressive male-male intolerance, extensive burrowing behaviour (burrows ~2 inches deep in sand/lava rock), molting frequency (juveniles often, adults 1–2× per year with pre-molt burrowing), pH sensitivity (loses colour and lethargy below ~7.8, optimal near 8.4), specialist snail-eater diet, and high climbing/escape ability.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Syntripsa
additional.notes: Taxonomic confirmation: Decapoda, Brachyura, Gecarcinucidae, subfamily Parathelphusinae. Genus Syntripsa erected by Chia & Ng, 2006; S. matannensis (Schenkel, 1902) is the type species; endemic to lakes on Sulawesi.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Juveniles molt frequently, while fully grown adults molt roughly every 8 weeks according to some sources — though specialist hobbyist accounts report only 1–2 molts per year in mature individuals. Pre-molt animals typically retreat into burrows for about a week and should not be disturbed during this period. Providing cuttlebone or another calcium source supports shell hardening after a molt. Unstable mineral content or sudden parameter swings can trigger failed molts, which are often fatal.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Reliable captive breeding has not been documented; only sporadic, unverified mating reports exist. Males are larger with proportionally bigger claws than females of similar age.
